ideal_answer: Python library for data manipulation and analysis. Core: Series (1D), DataFrame (2D table). Provides: SQL-like operations (filter, groupby, join, pivot), I/O (CSV, Excel, SQL, Parquet, JSON), time series support, missing data handling, statistical functions. Built on NumPy. Use for data cleaning, exploration, feature engineering. Performance: vectorized operations, avoid Python loops. For huge data: Polars (Rust-based, faster), Dask (parallel), PySpark. Critical skill for data roles.

question: Explain MLOps and its importance
ideal_answer: MLOps: DevOps for machine learning — practices for deploying, monitoring, maintaining ML models in production. Components: version control for code and data (DVC), experiment tracking (MLflow, Weights & Biases), model registry, automated training/retraining pipelines, deployment (FastAPI, KServe, SageMaker), monitoring (data drift, model drift, performance degradation), feedback loops. Importance: 87% of ML projects fail to reach production. MLOps bridges experimentation and production reality. Practices: CI/CD for ML, A/B testing models, canary deployments.

question: What is fine-tuning vs prompt engineering for LLMs?
ideal_answer: Fine-tuning: further train pre-trained model on domain-specific data, modifying weights. Expensive (GPUs needed), permanent changes. Use for: specialized tasks, domain language, behavior modification. Methods: full fine-tuning, LoRA (efficient adapter layers), QLoRA (quantized). Prompt engineering: craft input to steer LLM behavior without changing model. Cheap, flexible, immediate. Use for: most cases initially. RAG combines: retrieve context + prompt engineer. Order of preference: prompt eng → RAG → fine-tuning. Many tasks solvable with good prompting.

question: What are vector databases?
ideal_answer: Specialized databases for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors (embeddings). Use approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithms — HNSW, IVF, LSH — for fast similarity search at scale. Examples: FAISS (Meta, in-memory library), Pinecone (managed), Weaviate (open source), Milvus, Qdrant, ChromaDB. Compared to traditional DBs: exact search not feasible for vectors at scale. Use cases: RAG, semantic search, recommendation systems, image/video search, deduplication. Key metrics: recall (accuracy) vs latency vs throughput. Hybrid search combines vector + keyword.

question: Explain Docker containers vs virtual machines
ideal_answer: VMs: virtualize hardware, run separate OS, isolated but heavy (GBs, minutes to start). Examples: VMware, VirtualBox. Containers: virtualize OS, share host kernel, lightweight (MBs, seconds to start). Use Linux namespaces and cgroups for isolation. Examples: Docker, Podman. Containers more efficient, faster, portable. VMs better for: running different OS than host, stronger isolation (security), legacy apps. Modern: containers for stateless services, VMs for hypervisor-level isolation. Kubernetes orchestrates containers at scale.

question: What is Kubernetes and why use it?
ideal_answer: Container orchestration platform from Google. Manages container lifecycle: deployment, scaling, rolling updates, self-healing (restart failed pods), service discovery, load balancing. Core concepts: Pods (smallest unit, 1+ containers), Deployments (manage Pod replicas), Services (network access), Ingress (HTTP routing), ConfigMaps, Secrets, Volumes. Use when: many microservices, need auto-scaling, deploying across multi-cloud. Cons: operational complexity, learning curve, overhead. Alternatives: simpler PaaS (Heroku, Vercel) for small apps, ECS/Cloud Run for managed.

question: Explain CI/CD pipelines
ideal_answer: CI (Continuous Integration): frequently merge code with automated testing. CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment): automated release to staging (delivery) or production (deployment). Pipeline stages: source (git trigger), build (compile, package, container image), test (unit, integration, E2E), security scan, deploy. Tools: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI. Benefits: faster releases, less risk, automated quality checks, faster feedback. Best practices: fast pipelines (<10 min ideal), parallel jobs, environment promotion, automated rollbacks.

question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
ideal_answer: Managing infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) via code instead of manual config. Tools: Terraform (declarative, multi-cloud), CloudFormation (AWS-native), Pulumi (programming languages), Ansible (configuration management, also IaC). Benefits: version control, reproducible environments, code reviews, fast disaster recovery, environment parity. Patterns: modules for reusability, state management (remote backends), workspaces for environments. Best practices: small modules, plan before apply, no secrets in code, automated drift detection.

question: Explain AWS S3 and common use cases
ideal_answer: Object storage service: virtually unlimited storage of files (objects) in buckets. Each object has key, data, metadata. Storage classes: Standard (frequent access), Infrequent Access (cheaper, retrieval fee), Glacier (archive, hours retrieval). Use cases: static website hosting, backups, data lake, media storage, CDN origin. Features: versioning, encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS), lifecycle policies (auto-transition to cheaper storage), event triggers (Lambda), presigned URLs (temporary access), replication. Practically used in nearly every AWS architecture.

question: What is AWS Lambda and serverless?
ideal_answer: Lambda: run code without managing servers — provide function, AWS handles infrastructure. Triggers: API Gateway, S3 events, EventBridge, SQS. Pay per invocation + duration. Cold starts: latency on first call. Limits: 15-min runtime, 10GB memory. Use cases: event-driven processing, APIs, scheduled tasks, light workloads. Serverless extends to: SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, Step Functions. Frameworks: Serverless Framework, AWS SAM, CDK. Trade-offs: vendor lock-in, debugging complexity, cold starts. Cost-efficient at low/spiky traffic.

question: What is monitoring vs observability?
ideal_answer: Monitoring: checking known metrics for known problems (CPU, memory, request rate). Tools: Prometheus, Grafana, CloudWatch. Observability: understanding internal state from external outputs, including unknown problems. Three pillars: logs (events), metrics (numbers over time), traces (request flow across services). Tools: Datadog, New Relic, Honeycomb, OpenTelemetry (vendor-neutral). Modern systems need both: monitor for alerts, observe for debugging. SLO/SLI/error budgets quantify reliability targets.

question: Explain Prometheus and Grafana
ideal_answer: Prometheus: open-source time-series database for metrics. Pulls metrics from instrumented services via HTTP endpoint (/metrics). PromQL query language. Alerting via AlertManager. Service discovery for dynamic environments (Kubernetes). Grafana: visualization platform. Connects to Prometheus and other sources. Dashboards with panels (graphs, tables, alerts). Both common together in cloud-native stacks. Alternative: Datadog (managed), Victoria Metrics (Prometheus-compatible, more scalable), CloudWatch (AWS native).

question: What is GitOps?
ideal_answer: Operational pattern: Git as single source of truth for infrastructure and applications. Workflow: changes made via Git PRs, automated agents (ArgoCD, Flux) sync Git state to actual cluster. Benefits: audit trail, easy rollback (git revert), code review for infrastructure, declarative state. Pull-based vs traditional push-based deployment. Tools: ArgoCD (Kubernetes-focused), Flux (CNCF), Jenkins X. Combine with Helm/Kustomize for K8s manifests. Cultural shift: developers manage infrastructure through Git.

question: What is a service mesh?
ideal_answer: Infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication in microservices. Provides: load balancing, service discovery, mutual TLS (encryption + auth), retries, circuit breaking, observability — all without changing application code. Implementation: sidecar proxy (Envoy) injected next to each service. Tools: Istio (full-featured), Linkerd (simpler), Cilium (eBPF-based). Trade-offs: adds latency, operational complexity, debugging difficulty. Use when: many services, complex traffic patterns. Often overkill for small systems — simple API gateway sufficient.

question: What is the SLO, SLI, SLA framework?
ideal_answer: SLI (Service Level Indicator): measurable metric (e.g., latency, error rate, availability). SLO (Service Level Objective): target value for SLI (e.g., 99.9% requests under 100ms). SLA (Service Level Agreement): contractual obligation with consequences (refunds if missed) — usually less strict than SLO. Error budget: 1 - SLO (e.g., 0.1% = 43 min/month downtime). Use it for: balancing reliability vs feature velocity. Concept from Google SRE book. Don't over-engineer for unnecessary reliability.

question: What is chaos engineering?
ideal_answer: Discipline of experimenting on production systems to build confidence in their ability to withstand turbulent conditions. Originated at Netflix (Chaos Monkey). Process: form hypothesis (system continues under X failure), design experiment, run in production with limited blast radius, observe, learn, fix issues. Examples: kill random instances, inject latency, fill disks, terminate network. Tools: Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, AWS FIS. Required for: distributed systems where failures are inevitable. Start in staging, build confidence to production.

question: What is the OWASP Top 10?
ideal_answer: OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) ranks most critical web app security risks. 2021 list includes: A01 Broken Access Control (most common), A02 Cryptographic Failures, A03 Injection (SQL, XSS), A04 Insecure Design, A05 Security Misconfiguration, A06 Vulnerable Components, A07 Identification/Auth Failures, A08 Software and Data Integrity Failures, A09 Logging/Monitoring Failures, A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). Standard reference for security training, audits, and prioritization. Updated every few years.

question: Explain symmetric vs asymmetric encryption
ideal_answer: Symmetric: same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast, used for bulk data. Examples: AES (current standard), ChaCha20. Challenge: securely sharing the key. Asymmetric (public-key): different keys for encryption and decryption. Slow, used for key exchange and signatures. Examples: RSA (factoring), ECDSA, Ed25519 (elliptic curves). Public key shareable, private key secret. TLS uses both: asymmetric for handshake/key exchange, symmetric for actual data transfer. Hashing (SHA-256, bcrypt, Argon2) is one-way and different.

question: What is the difference between hashing and encryption?
ideal_answer: Encryption: reversible — can decrypt back to original with key. Used for: protecting data in transit/at rest. Examples: AES, RSA. Hashing: one-way — cannot reverse to original. Used for: password storage, integrity verification, deduplication. Properties: deterministic, fixed output size, avalanche effect (small change → big difference). Examples: SHA-256, SHA-3, bcrypt, Argon2 (designed for passwords with salt). Never use general hashes (MD5, SHA-1) for passwords — use Argon2/bcrypt/scrypt with built-in salting and slow computation.

question: Explain TLS/SSL handshake
ideal_answer: TLS handshake establishes secure connection. Simplified: 1) Client hello (supported ciphers, random number). 2) Server hello (chosen cipher, random number, certificate with public key). 3) Client verifies certificate via CA chain, generates pre-master secret, encrypts with server's public key, sends. 4) Both derive symmetric session key from random numbers and pre-master secret. 5) Subsequent communication uses symmetric encryption (much faster). TLS 1.3 simplified this to 1-RTT (round-trip time). Provides: confidentiality, integrity, server authentication (and optionally client).

question: What is OAuth 2.0 vs OpenID Connect?
ideal_answer: OAuth 2.0: authorization framework — granting access to resources without sharing credentials. About 'what can this app do on my behalf'. OpenID Connect (OIDC): identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 — adds authentication ('who is this user'). OIDC returns ID Token (JWT with user info) alongside access token. Use OAuth for: third-party app authorization (post to Twitter on my behalf). Use OIDC for: SSO, 'Login with Google'. Often confused — OAuth alone isn't authentication. Implementations: Auth0, Okta, AWS Cognito, Keycloak.

question: What is a firewall and types of firewalls?
ideal_answer: Firewall filters network traffic based on rules. Types: Packet-filtering (stateless, basic) — examines individual packets. Stateful (most common) — tracks connection state. Application-layer (Layer 7) — understands HTTP/SMTP/etc., used for WAFs. Next-generation firewalls (NGFW): include intrusion prevention, deep packet inspection, malware detection. Network firewall vs Web Application Firewall (WAF): WAF specifically for web apps (SQL injection, XSS). Cloud: AWS Security Groups, NACLs, WAF. Defense in depth: multiple firewall layers.

question: Explain zero trust architecture
ideal_answer: Security model: 'never trust, always verify'. Traditional: trust everything inside network perimeter. Zero trust: no implicit trust based on location. Principles: verify explicitly (every request), least privilege access, assume breach. Implementation: identity-based access (instead of IP-based), continuous authentication, micro-segmentation, encrypted everything, monitor all access. BeyondCorp (Google) pioneered. Tools: Cloudflare Access, Tailscale, Twingate. Increasingly important with remote work, cloud, BYOD eroding traditional perimeter.

question: What is penetration testing?
ideal_answer: Authorized simulated cyberattack to evaluate security. Phases: 1) Planning and reconnaissance, 2) Scanning (Nmap, vulnerability scanners), 3) Gaining access (exploit vulnerabilities), 4) Maintaining access, 5) Analysis and reporting. Types: Black box (no info given), White box (full info), Gray box (some info). Categories: external, internal, web app, mobile, social engineering, physical. Frameworks: PTES, OWASP Testing Guide. Tools: Burp Suite, Metasploit, Nmap, Wireshark. Different from bug bounty (continuous, crowdsourced) and red team (longer-term, undetected).

question: What is DDoS and how to mitigate?
ideal_answer: DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service): overwhelm service with traffic from many sources, making it unavailable. Types: Volume (bandwidth), Protocol (TCP SYN floods), Application (HTTP floods, Slowloris). Mitigation layers: CDN/Cloudflare (absorbs attacks at edge), rate limiting, IP blocking, traffic scrubbing services (Akamai, AWS Shield), WAF rules, auto-scaling (with cost monitoring), Anycast routing (distributes load). Detect: traffic anomalies, error rates. Modern attacks: amplification (DNS, NTP), application-layer harder to detect.

question: What is GDPR and key compliance requirements?
ideal_answer: EU regulation protecting personal data of EU residents. Applies to any org handling EU user data. Key principles: lawfulness (legal basis required), purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, security, accountability. User rights: access, rectification, erasure ('right to be forgotten'), portability, restriction, objection. Requirements: privacy by design, breach notification (72 hours), DPO if scale requires, DPIA for high-risk processing, consent must be specific and revocable. Fines up to 4% global revenue. Similar laws: CCPA (California), LGPD (Brazil).

question: Tell me about yourself
ideal_answer: Use the present-past-future structure: brief background, current role/achievements relevant to the position, future aspirations aligning with this role. Keep it 1-2 minutes focused on professional journey. Example: 'I'm a full-stack developer with 3 years of experience building scalable web applications. Currently at [company] where I led the migration to microservices architecture, improving deployment speed by 60%. Looking to grow into a senior engineering role where I can contribute to AI/ML products, which aligns with what your team is building.' Avoid personal life details unless asked.

question: What are your greatest strengths?
ideal_answer: Choose 2-3 strengths with concrete examples. Pick strengths relevant to the role. Example: 'My greatest strengths are problem decomposition and learning agility. When faced with our payment system's reliability issue, I systematically broke it down — identified that 80% of failures came from one specific edge case in webhook handling. I quickly learned the third-party API documentation and implemented a fix in two days that reduced failed transactions by 95%. I also stay current with industry trends — recently completed an LLM systems course to apply RAG patterns to our internal docs search.' Avoid clichés like 'I'm a perfectionist'.

question: What is your greatest weakness?
ideal_answer: Choose a real weakness you're actively improving — avoid 'I work too hard'. Show self-awareness and growth. Example: 'My weakness has been over-engineering solutions. Early in my career, I'd build elaborate abstractions for problems that didn't need them, slowing down delivery. I've worked on this by adopting YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) principle, starting with simpler solutions, and only refactoring when patterns emerge from real requirements. My team's recent feedback was that my code is now appropriately simple while remaining extensible. I still consciously check myself when designing systems.' Demonstrate concrete improvement steps and results.

question: Why do you want to work here?
ideal_answer: Research the company beforehand. Connect their mission/products/tech/culture to your interests. Be specific. Example: 'Three things attract me to [Company]. First, your work on [specific product/initiative] aligns with my passion for solving [problem domain]. Second, your engineering blog posts about [specific technical practice] show a culture of technical excellence I want to be part of. Third, the team structure you described — small autonomous teams owning products end-to-end — matches how I work best. Plus, [specific person]'s recent talk on [topic] convinced me of the team's depth.' Avoid generic answers like 'great company culture'.

question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
ideal_answer: Show ambition aligned with realistic growth at this company. Avoid 'in your job' (threatening) or 'I don't know' (unmotivated). Example: 'In five years, I want to be a senior engineer who can architect complex systems and mentor others. I see myself going deep in [specific technical area like distributed systems / ML platforms] while also developing my leadership skills — perhaps as a tech lead. I'd like to be the person teams come to for solving difficult problems. This role offers exactly the right challenges and learning environment to build toward that.' Tie to the company's growth opportunities.

question: Why are you leaving your current job?
ideal_answer: Be positive — never bash former employer. Focus on what's pulling you toward the new role, not pushing you away. Example: 'I've grown significantly in my current role over the past 3 years — I learned [specific skills] and led [specific projects]. However, the next stage of my growth requires exposure to [specific challenge: bigger scale / new domain / leadership / different tech]. Your role offers exactly that. My current company is good, but they're focused on [different area] which limits the growth opportunities in my chosen direction.' Even if leaving for negative reasons, frame around positive next steps.

question: Describe a time you failed
ideal_answer: Choose a real failure showing reflection and growth — not too damaging, not trivial. STAR format. Example: 'In my previous role, I led the migration to a new caching layer. I underestimated the complexity of stale data issues and didn't build sufficient monitoring. Two weeks after launch, we discovered users seeing outdated data for hours. We had to roll back, costing two weeks of work. From this I learned: (1) always build observability before deploying critical infrastructure, (2) phased rollouts with monitoring, (3) consult engineers with deeper expertise upfront. My next migration was successful, with comprehensive monitoring and gradual rollout.' Demonstrate self-awareness, lessons learned, and applied changes.

question: Describe a time you handled conflict with a coworker
ideal_answer: Show professional conflict resolution. STAR format. Example: 'A senior engineer and I disagreed on the database design for a new feature. They wanted denormalized for performance; I wanted normalized for data integrity. The disagreement was blocking sprint planning. I scheduled a 1:1 to understand their concerns deeply. They were worried about query performance based on a past incident. I proposed a compromise: start normalized with specific indexes, benchmark, and denormalize specific hot paths if needed. We agreed, implemented it, and the design has worked well for 18 months. I learned: seek to understand the underlying concern, not just the position.' Show empathy, active listening, and compromise.

question: Tell me about a time you had to learn something quickly
ideal_answer: Show learning agility and self-direction. STAR format. Example: 'When my team adopted Kubernetes, I had no prior experience but needed to deploy a critical service in three weeks. I created a structured learning plan: official docs in week 1, hands-on labs in week 2, real implementation in week 3. I also identified two team members with expertise and scheduled 30-minute sessions weekly to validate my understanding. I deployed the service on time with proper monitoring and documentation. I've used this same approach to learn React, GraphQL, and recently LLMs — break it down, hands-on practice, validate with experts.' Demonstrate a repeatable learning system.

question: How do you handle tight deadlines?
ideal_answer: Show prioritization, communication, and quality awareness. Example: 'I follow a few principles. First, identify the MVP — what's the minimum that delivers value. Cut nice-to-haves ruthlessly. Second, communicate early — if I see a risk to deadline, I raise it immediately with options. Third, get help — pair program on hard parts, delegate where possible. Fourth, never sacrifice on tests for critical paths — tech debt from skipped tests costs more later. For example, our team had a 2-week deadline for a feature originally scoped for 4. I led scoping it down to core flows, communicated trade-offs to stakeholders, and we shipped on time with the must-haves and a clear backlog for the rest.'

question: What questions do you have for us?
ideal_answer: Always have prepared, thoughtful questions — never 'no questions'. Good questions show research and engagement. Examples: (1) Team and culture: 'How does the team handle technical decisions — top-down or collaborative?' (2) Growth: 'What does career growth look like for someone in this role?' (3) Challenges: 'What's the biggest technical challenge the team is facing right now?' (4) Success: 'In 6 months, what would success look like for this position?' (5) Process: 'How does the team balance shipping features with addressing technical debt?' Avoid asking what's clearly on website. Tailor questions to the interviewer's role.

question: What are your salary expectations?
ideal_answer: Research market rates first (Glassdoor, levels.fyi, Blind). Provide a range based on research, not a single number. Example: 'Based on my research of comparable roles in this market and my experience with [relevant skills], I'm targeting the [X-Y] range. However, I'm open to discussing the full compensation package including equity, benefits, and growth opportunities. What's the budgeted range for this role?' Always tie expectations to research. Try to get them to share their range first if possible. Don't undersell yourself — negotiate from a position of knowledge.

question: Describe a time you took initiative
ideal_answer: Show proactivity beyond assigned work. STAR format. Example: 'I noticed our deployment process took 45 minutes per release, causing frequent context switches and missed deployments. Nobody had asked me to fix it — I owned a feature team, not infrastructure. I prototyped a faster pipeline using parallelized stages and pre-built Docker images. Showed the team a 10-minute deployment proof of concept. Got buy-in, then refined it with the DevOps team. Result: deployment time dropped from 45 to 8 minutes, releases per week tripled. The pattern was adopted by other teams. I learned that solving cross-cutting problems creates outsized impact.'

question: How do you mentor junior developers?
ideal_answer: Show patience, structure, and growth orientation. Example: 'I mentor based on the person and topic. For new hires, I pair-program on their first features to demonstrate codebase patterns. I prefer questions over answers — asking 'what do you think happens here?' builds their reasoning. I do code reviews as teaching moments — explain why, not just what to change. I set up regular 1:1s focused on their growth, not just tasks. I share resources tailored to their gaps: courses, articles, conference talks. Most importantly, I create safety to ask 'stupid' questions. My current mentee shipped their first major feature solo last month. Mentoring also keeps me learning — explaining sharpens understanding.'

question: Describe a project you led from start to finish
ideal_answer: Show ownership, planning, execution, and learning. STAR format. Example: 'I led the migration from monolith to microservices for our user management system. Started by aligning with product on goals — independent deployments and team autonomy. Designed the architecture with input from 3 senior engineers. Created a 6-week rollout plan with weekly milestones. Coordinated with 4 teams whose services depended on user data. Set up shared infrastructure: API gateway, service mesh, centralized auth. Conducted weekly demos to stakeholders. We launched on time, achieved 80% reduction in cross-team deployment dependencies. Post-launch retrospective identified 3 improvements for next migration. I learned the importance of stakeholder communication — half the work was alignment, not code.'

question: How do you give and receive feedback?
ideal_answer: Show emotional intelligence and growth mindset. Example: 'When giving feedback, I use specific examples and focus on behavior, not personality. I follow SBI: Situation, Behavior, Impact. I give it timely — not weeks after the fact. I balance positive and constructive — recognizing good work makes corrective feedback land better. When receiving, I assume positive intent, ask clarifying questions, and acknowledge the feedback. I separate the message from my ego — feedback is data, not judgment. I follow up by demonstrating change. My manager once said my PRs were missing context — I now write detailed PR descriptions, and reviews are 50% faster. Both giving and receiving feedback well are core to team trust.'

question: What is React Native and how does it work?
ideal_answer: React Native: framework for building mobile apps with JavaScript/TypeScript and React. Compiles to native UI components (not WebView) using a JavaScript-to-native bridge. Architecture: JS thread runs React, native threads handle UI. Components map to native equivalents (View → UIView/ViewGroup). New architecture (Fabric) replaces bridge with JSI (JavaScript Interface) for synchronous calls and better performance. Use for cross-platform mobile with code sharing 70-90% between iOS/Android. Trade-offs vs native: slower platform-specific features, performance overhead, larger app size.

question: What is Flutter and how does it differ from React Native?
ideal_answer: Flutter: Google's UI framework using Dart language. Draws own UI via Skia engine — no native components, ensuring consistent look across platforms. Hot reload for fast development. React Native uses native components via bridge. Flutter pros: consistent UI, single rendering engine, better performance (no bridge), strong tooling. RN pros: JavaScript ecosystem, mature OSS, looks native by default, larger community. Dart faster than JavaScript. Flutter better for design-heavy apps with custom UI; RN for teams with web/React background.

question: How do you handle navigation in React Native?
ideal_answer: Primary library: React Navigation. Types: Stack Navigator (push/pop screens), Tab Navigator (bottom tabs), Drawer Navigator (side menu), Modal. Configure in navigation container at app root. Pass params between screens via navigation.navigate('Screen', { id }). Deep linking for URL → screen mapping. Native stack (uses native navigation primitives) for better performance than JS stack. Authentication flow: conditional navigators based on auth state. Type-safe with TypeScript generics. Alternative: react-native-navigation (Wix) for fully native navigation.

question: Explain mobile push notifications
ideal_answer: Server sends notification to platform service (FCM for Android, APNs for iOS) which delivers to device. Components: 1) Device registers, gets unique token, sends to your server. 2) Server stores tokens per user. 3) Server sends payload to FCM/APNs. 4) Platform delivers to device. 5) App handles foreground/background/killed states differently. iOS needs explicit user permission (since iOS 12, optional on Android). Payloads: alert (visible), data-only (silent). Services: Firebase Cloud Messaging is most common; OneSignal, Pusher for managed. Best practice: rich notifications with images, actions; don't spam users.

question: What is offline-first design for mobile apps?
ideal_answer: Design assuming network may be unavailable. Strategies: 1) Cache aggressively with stale-while-revalidate. 2) Local DB (SQLite, Realm, AsyncStorage for small data) as source of truth. 3) Queue actions when offline, sync when online. 4) Optimistic UI updates — show changes immediately, reconcile later. 5) Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins, custom merge). 6) Background sync. Libraries: WatermelonDB, RxDB, PowerSync. Considerations: data freshness vs offline capability, conflict resolution, sync indicators in UI. Critical for mobile UX in areas with poor connectivity.
